title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a master bookbinder called Ethan Thorne talking about the craft of manual bookbinding in the digital age. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a master bookbinder called Ethan Thorne talking about the craft of manual bookbinding in the digital age. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: Ethan Thorne discusses the materials, techniques, and the cultural value of handmade books.
extracts.audio_script:
Hello, I’m Ethan. In an era where thousands of books can fit onto a single electronic device, you might wonder why anyone still binds books by hand. My journey into this craft didn't start in a library; it actually began when I found a [7]tattered family Bible[/7] in my grandmother's attic. The leather was crumbling, and the pages were falling out, but the history it held was palpable. I wanted to learn how to save it, and that curiosity turned into a lifelong career.

Manual bookbinding is a slow, methodical process that hasn't changed much in centuries. The first step is always preparing the 'sections' or groups of folded pages. I’ve found that the [8]thread tension[/8] during the sewing process is the most critical factor for the book's longevity. If it’s too tight, the paper will tear; if it’s too loose, the book won't open flat. It requires a very sensitive touch that only comes with years of practice.

Materials are also vital. We use archival-quality papers and specialized glues. I’ve noticed that [9]wheat starch paste[/9] is still the best adhesive for delicate repairs. Unlike modern synthetic glues, it’s reversible, meaning a future conservator can remove it without damaging the original paper. We are always thinking about how the book will look and feel in another hundred years.

The most decorative part of the process is 'tooling' the leather cover with gold leaf. I’ve found that the [10]tool temperature[/10] is the biggest challenge during this stage. If the brass tool is too hot, it burns the leather; if it’s too cold, the gold won't stick properly. You have to judge the heat by the sound the tool makes when you touch it with a wet sponge. It’s a sensory experience that combines sight, sound, and touch.

We are also seeing a rise in 'artistic binding', where the cover reflects the theme of the book. I recently worked on a project where I used [11]reclaimed wood[/11] for the covers of a book about forest conservation. It made the object itself a piece of sculpture. People are increasingly seeing books not just as containers for information, but as physical works of art that they want to display in their homes.

However, finding high-quality materials is becoming harder. I’m worried that the [12]limited supply[/12] of ethically sourced goatskin will eventually force us to change our traditional methods. Many of the small tanneries we rely on are closing down as demand shifts toward mass-produced synthetic alternatives. It’s a threat to the authenticity of the craft.

Looking back, the biggest change I’ve seen is in the profile of our students. I noticed that [13]graphic designers[/13] are now the largest group interested in learning traditional binding. They spend all day in front of screens and they are desperate for a tactile, physical connection to their work. They want to understand the 'physics' of a book.

Finally, I’m currently developing an [14]online workshop[/14] that focuses on simple binding techniques for beginners. I want to show people that they can create something beautiful and lasting with just a few basic tools. Thank you for your time.

dialogue.speaker: ethan
